ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers daikon/Chinese radish as a food item; boiled preparation is captured in classification.preparation_method.

main_ingredients: Chinese radish (daikon, Raphanus sativus var. longipinnatus)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled Chinese radish is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl served as a side dish or soup component.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled Chinese Radish — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled Chinese radish (F14040027). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on raw daikon/white radish GI ≈ 15 (low-GI root vegetable, boiling does not substantially raise GI for this low-starch vegetable)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for boiled Chinese radish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on the closest analogous entry for raw/cooked white radish (daikon) in the international GI tables. Boiling a low-starch, high-water-content root vegetable does not materially raise GI. GL per 150 g serving = (15 × 3.43 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.77, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (13 mg/100 g) and high potassium (285 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled Chinese radish is highly suitable for hypertension management: its sodium content of 13 mg/100 g is negligible and its potassium-to-sodium ratio exceeds 20:1, actively supporting vasodilation and blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.43 g/100 g), low sugar (1.83 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled Chinese radish is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate load, low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 15, and 1.6 g/100 g dietary fibre which slows glucose absorption and supports postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled Chinese radish poses no cardiovascular risk: saturated fat is negligible at 0.03 g/100 g, cholesterol is zero, and its high potassium content (285 mg/100 g) supports arterial health, making it a heart-healthy vegetable suitable for regular inclusion in a cardioprotective diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C (~15 mg/100 g) and isothiocyanate anti-inflammatory compounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled Chinese radish is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis; its vitamin C content supports collagen synthesis and antioxidant defence, while naturally occurring isothiocyanates provide mild anti-inflammatory activity with negligible saturated fat and zero trans fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled Chinese radish (白萝卜, daikon) is a minimally processed vegetable dish prepared by boiling the white root of Raphanus sativus var. longipinnatus until tender. The result is a soft, mildly sweet, and subtly peppery vegetable with a high water content and very low caloric density of only 17 kcal per 100 g. It is widely consumed across Chinese, Singaporean, Japanese, and Korean cuisines as a side dish, soup ingredient, or stew component. Nutritionally, boiled Chinese radish is notable for its very low carbohydrate content (3.43 g/100 g), low sugar (1.83 g/100 g), moderate dietary fibre (1.6 g/100 g), and exceptionally low sodium (13 mg/100 g) paired with high potassium (285 mg/100 g). These properties make it an excellent choice for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Its estimated glycaemic index of approximately 15 places it firmly in the low-GI category. The vegetable also provides vitamin C (~15 mg/100 g) and trace iron (0.15 mg/100 g). It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is suitable for low-sodium, low-calorie, and ketogenic dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Is boiled Chinese radish good for diabetes?

What is the GI of boiled white radish?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14040027) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / international GI tables for white radish, which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Saturated fat value (0.03 g/100 g) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for boiled daikon (NDB 11429).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for boiled Chinese radish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14040027) or USDA FoodData Central boiled daikon entry as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source record. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed zero for a plain boiled vegetable with no added fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source; naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on white radish/daikon in the University of Sydney GI Database international tables; no direct boiled Chinese radish GI entry exists in approved sources. Saturated fat of 0.03 g/100 g cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11429, boiled daikon). Vitamin C of 15 mg/100 g is an estimate consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for boiled daikon; the HPB source does not report vitamin C directly.
